libraryDescription:
The VCSSL Math library is a standard library providing mathematical functions.
Specifically, it includes exponential functions, logarithmic functions, square roots and n-th roots, factorials, trigonometric functions, inverse trigonometric functions, hyperbolic functions, inverse hyperbolic functions, and absolute-value and max/min functions.

This library provides mathematical functions in both 64-bit float precision and arbitrary precision. The former is handled as the float type, and the latter as the varfloat type.
In VCSSL, the float type is not distinguished from the double type. Both have the precision defined by the implementation, and in the current official implementation they are IEEE754 64-bit floats.
As for the varfloat type, the current official implementation does not use a unified standard specification.Instead, it adopts a proprietary decimal arbitrary-precision specification. Rounding is performed by truncation in decimal arithmetic.
The detailed specification of the varfloat type depends on the implementation, and may be changed to some unified standard in the future.

For the float (double) implementations of mathematical functions, built-in implementation features are used, and they are handled at runtime as a kind of special arithmetic instruction.
Many of the float implementations of mathematical functions provided by the Math library are function macros wrapping such special arithmetic instructions.
Therefore, they have no function-call overhead and generally provide good performance.

In contrast, the varfloat (arbitrary-precision) implementations of mathematical functions are implemented in VCSSL code.
Specifically, the algorithms for obtaining values are all implemented in VCSSL using the varfloat type, by methods such as Maclaurin expansion, Taylor expansion, Newton's method, and the Gauss-Legendre algorithm.
For this reason, the execution speed is generally in the slow category. This is due to factors such as the implementation being written in a scripting language, and the varfloat type itself being slow to operate on.
As a concrete example, the following code calculates sin(1) with 1000 significant digits. In this calculation, the sin function takes just under one second to execute.

digit( 1000 ); // Set the number of significant digits to 1000
varfloat value = sin( 1.0vf ); // Calculate the sin function
print( value ); // Output

In the varfloat implementations of mathematical functions, many depend on convergence algorithms, so some accumulated error may appear in the trailing digits.
Anticipating this, the functions internally calculate using a somewhat larger number of digits, varying by function, and truncate the extra digits when returning the value.
However, this is only an auxiliary measure and does not guarantee that digits affected by accumulated error are always eliminated for every possible number of significant digits and every argument.
Depending on the function, validation of accumulated error is generally performed assuming uses of a few dozen significant digits and arguments within ordinary ranges.
For uses requiring thousands to tens of thousands of digits, or for arguments near singular points, accumulated error generally becomes larger and may not fit within the truncated extra digits, so it can also appear in the trailing digits of the returned value.
Therefore, for such uses, please also use a sufficiently large number of significant digits on the caller side while taking accumulated error into account, and take measures such as truncating extra digits.

variable0.name: PI
variable0.description: The value of pi in float precision. To obtain a varfloat value in arbitrary precision, use pi().

variable1.name: E
variable1.description: The value of Euler's number, the base of natural logarithms, in float precision. To obtain a varfloat value in arbitrary precision, calculate it with exp( 1.0vf ).

function0.name: nan
function0.description: Determines whether a float value is NaN.
function0.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be checked.
function0.returnValue: Returns true if it is NaN, or false otherwise.
function0.example: float x = 0.0/0.0; bool isNaN = nan(x); print("isNaN=" + isNaN);

function1.name: inf
function1.description: Determines whether a float value is Inf.
function1.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be checked.
function1.returnValue: Returns true if it is Inf, or false otherwise.
function1.example: float x = 1.0/0.0; bool isInf = inf(x); print("isInf=" + isInf);

function2.name: and
function2.declaration: macro and( int number1, int number2 )
function2.description: Calculates the bitwise AND operation on int values.
function2.parameters:
(int type) number1 : A value to be operated on. 
(int type) number2 : A value to be operated on.
function2.example: int a = 0b01010101; int b = 0b11110000; int c = and(a, b); print( bin(c) ); // 0b1010000

function3.name: or
function3.declaration: macro or( int number1, int number2 )
function3.description: Calculates the bitwise OR operation on int values.
function3.parameters:
(int type) number1 : A value to be operated on. 
(int type) number2 : A value to be operated on.

function4.name: xor
function4.declaration: macro xor( int number1, int number2 )
function4.description: Calculates the bitwise XOR operation on int values.
function4.parameters:
(int type) number1 : A value to be operated on. 
(int type) number2 : A value to be operated on.
function4.example: int a = 0b01010101; int b = 0b11110000; int c = xor(a, b); print( bin(c) ); // 0b10100101

function5.name: random
function5.description: Returns a value greater than or equal to 0.0 and less than 1.0. Note that the documentation before VCSSL 3.0.20 stated that it "returns a random value from 0.0 to 1.0," but that expression was ambiguous, and in practice a value exactly equal to 1.0 is never returned.

function6.name: rand
function6.description: Equivalent to random. The use of random is currently recommended instead. Because the name of this function can cause confusion with integer random numbers, its use is no longer recommended. In the future, changing the type of this function may be considered.

function7.name: pi
function7.description:
Calculates the value of pi.
Internally, it is calculated by the Gauss-Legendre algorithm using 1.1 times the original number of digits.
A high-precision value of pi is required when calculating periodic functions or simulations with periodic boundaries, such as rotational systems and mapping systems, in order to obtain remainders of arguments or coordinates modulo pi.In fact, the Math library itself uses it for calculations such as varfloat trigonometric functions.

function8.name: exp
function8.description: Calculates the exponential function with Euler's number as the base.
function8.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function9.name: exp
function9.description:
Calculates the exponential function with Euler's number as the base.
Internally, it is calculated by Maclaurin expansion using 1.34 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4.This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function9.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function10.name: pow
function10.declaration: macro pow( float arg1, float arg2 )
function10.parameters:
(float type) arg1 : The base. 
(float type) arg2 : The exponent.

function11.name: pow
function11.declaration: varfloat pow( varfloat arg1, varint arg2 )
function11.description:
Calculates a power whose exponent is an integer.
Internally, it is calculated by repeated multiplication using 2.1 times the number of digits given by "the original number of digits plus the absolute value of the order of the exponent (arg2)."However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4.This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function11.parameters:
(varfloat type) arg1 : The base. 
(varint type) arg2 : The exponent.

function12.name: pow
function12.declaration: varfloat pow( varfloat arg1, varfloat arg2 )
function12.description:
Calculates a power whose exponent is a floating-point number, that is, a real number.
Internally, it is calculated using 1.2 times the number of digits given by "the original number of digits plus the absolute value of the order of the exponent (arg2)," by either Maclaurin expansion or a combination of exponential and logarithmic functions.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4.This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function12.parameters:
(varfloat type) arg1 : The base. 
(varfloat type) arg2 : The exponent.

function13.name: pow
function13.declaration: varfloat pow( varint arg1, varfloat arg2 )
function13.description:
Calculates a power.
Internally, arg1 is cast to varfloat and then pow( varfloat, varfloat ) is used.
function13.parameters:
(varint type) arg1 : The base. 
(varfloat type) arg2 : The exponent.

function14.name: ln
function14.description: Calculates the natural logarithm. It is the same as log.
function14.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function15.name: ln
function15.description:
Calculates the natural logarithm. It is the same as log.
Internally, log(varfloat) is used as is.
function15.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function16.name: log
function16.description: Calculates the natural logarithm. It is the same as ln.
function16.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function17.name: log
function17.description:
Calculates the natural logarithm. It is the same as ln.
Internally, it is calculated using 1.34 times the original number of digits, by Newton's method for arguments greater than or equal to 1, and by Taylor expansion for arguments less than or equal to 1.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4.This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function17.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function18.name: log10
function18.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function19.name: log10
function19.description:
Calculates the common logarithm.
Internally, it is calculated by "log10(x) = ln(x) / ln(10)" using 2.2 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function19.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function20.name: sqrt
function20.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function21.name: sqrt
function21.description:
Calculates the square root.
Internally, it is calculated by Newton's method using 1.34 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function21.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function22.name: root
function22.declaration: macro root( float arg, int rootPower )
function22.parameters:
(float type) arg : The value to be calculated. 
(int type) rootPower : The n in the n-th root.

function23.name: root
function23.declaration: varfloat root( varfloat arg, varint p )
function23.description:
Calculates the n-th root.
Internally, it is calculated by Newton's method using 1.34 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function23.parameters:
(varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated. 
(varint type) p : The n in the n-th root.

function24.name: root
function24.declaration: varfloat root( varfloat arg, varfloat vfp )
function24.description:
Calculates the n-th root.
Internally, it is calculated by Newton's method using 1.34 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using the original number of digits plus 4. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.Note that the second argument of this function is always regarded as an integer.This function is supported only for convenience in modes such as calculator software, where numeric literals are regarded as floating-point numbers.Since there is no particular advantage to using it in ordinary situations, please use root( varfloat, varint ).
function24.parameters:
(varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated. 
(varfloat type) vfp : The n in the n-th root.

function25.name: abs
function25.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function26.name: abs
function26.parameters: (int type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function27.name: abs
function27.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function28.name: abs
function28.parameters: (varint type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function29.name: max
function29.declaration: macro max( float arg1, float arg2 )
function29.description: Returns the larger of the two arguments.
function29.parameters:
(float type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(float type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function30.name: max
function30.declaration: macro max( int arg1, int arg2 )
function30.description: Returns the larger of the two arguments.
function30.parameters:
(int type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(int type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function31.name: max
function31.declaration: varfloat max( varfloat arg1, varfloat arg2 )
function31.description: Returns the larger of the two arguments.
function31.parameters:
(varfloat type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(varfloat type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function32.name: max
function32.declaration: varint max( varint arg1, varint arg2 )
function32.description: Returns the larger of the two arguments.
function32.parameters:
(varint type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(varint type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function33.name: min
function33.declaration: macro min( float arg1, float arg2 )
function33.description: Returns the smaller of the two arguments.
function33.parameters:
(float type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(float type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function34.name: min
function34.declaration: macro min( int arg1, int arg2 )
function34.description: Returns the smaller of the two arguments.
function34.parameters:
(int type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(int type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function35.name: min
function35.declaration: varfloat min( varfloat arg1, varfloat arg2 )
function35.description: Returns the smaller of the two arguments.
function35.parameters:
(varfloat type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(varfloat type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function36.name: min
function36.declaration: varint min( varint arg1, varint arg2 )
function36.description: Returns the smaller of the two arguments.
function36.parameters:
(varint type) arg1 : A value to be calculated. 
(varint type) arg2 : A value to be calculated.

function37.name: fac
function37.description: Calculates the factorial. Although both the argument and the return value of this function are float, the calculation is performed after internally rounding to a nearby int. The conversion may affect the precision because of rounding error. If strict exactness is required, use the version taking an int argument.
function37.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function38.name: fac
function38.parameters: (int type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function39.name: fac
function39.parameters: (varint type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function40.name: fac
function40.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function41.name: sin
function41.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function42.name: sin
function42.description:
Calculates the sine function.
Internally, it is calculated by Maclaurin expansion using "1.34 times the original number of digits plus 10 times the order of the argument" digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4, plus 10 times the order of the argument" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
For arguments outside the range from 0 to pi/2, the symmetry and periodicity of trigonometric functions are used, and internally the calculation is performed using values in the range from 0 to pi.
function42.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function43.name: cos
function43.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function44.name: cos
function44.description:
Calculates the cosine function.
Internally, it is calculated by Maclaurin expansion using "1.34 times the original number of digits plus 10 times the order of the argument" digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4, plus 10 times the order of the argument" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
For arguments outside the range from 0 to pi/2, the symmetry and periodicity of trigonometric functions are used, and internally the calculation is performed using values in the range from 0 to pi.
function44.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function45.name: tan
function45.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function46.name: tan
function46.description:
Calculates the tangent function.
Internally, it is calculated by "tan(x) = sin(x) / cos(x)" using 2.1 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function46.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function47.name: asin
function47.description: Calculates the arcsine function, which is the inverse function of sine.
function47.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function48.name: asin
function48.description:
Calculates the arcsine function, which is the inverse function of sine.
Internally, it is calculated using 1.34 times the original number of digits, by Maclaurin expansion for arguments whose absolute value is 0.7 or less, and by Newton's method outside that range.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function48.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function49.name: acos
function49.description: Calculates the arccosine function, which is the inverse function of cosine.
function49.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function50.name: acos
function50.description:
Calculates the arccosine function, which is the inverse function of cosine.
Internally, it is calculated by "acos(x) = pi/2 - asin(x)" using 1.34 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function50.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function51.name: atan
function51.description: Calculates the arctangent function, which is the inverse function of tangent.
function51.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function52.name: atan
function52.description:
Calculates the arctangent function, which is the inverse function of tangent.
Internally, it is calculated using 1.34 times the original number of digits, by Maclaurin expansion for arguments whose absolute value is 0.7 or less, and by Newton's method outside that range.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function52.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function53.name: sinh
function53.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function54.name: sinh
function54.description:
Calculates the hyperbolic sine function.
Internally, it is calculated by "sinh(x) = ( exp(x) - exp(-x) ) / 2" using 2.1 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function54.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function55.name: cosh
function55.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function56.name: cosh
function56.description:
Calculates the hyperbolic cosine function.
Internally, it is calculated by "cosh(x) = ( exp(x) + exp(-x) ) / 2" using 2.1 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function56.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function57.name: tanh
function57.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function58.name: tanh
function58.description:
Calculates the hyperbolic tangent function.
Internally, it is calculated by "tanh(x) = ( exp(x) - exp(-x) ) / ( exp(x) + exp(-x) )" using 2.8 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function58.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function59.name: asinh
function59.description: Calculates the inverse hyperbolic sine function.
function59.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function60.name: asinh
function60.description:
Calculates the inverse hyperbolic sine function.
Internally, it is calculated by "asinh(x) = ln( x + sqrt( x*x + 1 ) )" using 2.1 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function60.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function61.name: acosh
function61.description: Calculates the inverse hyperbolic cosine function.
function61.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function62.name: acosh
function62.description:
Calculates the inverse hyperbolic cosine function.
Internally, it is calculated by "acosh(x) = ln( x + sqrt( x*x - 1 ) )" using 2.1 times the original number of digits.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.
function62.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function63.name: atanh
function63.description: Calculates the inverse hyperbolic tangent function.
function63.parameters: (float type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function64.name: atanh
function64.description:
Calculates the inverse hyperbolic tangent function.
Internally, it is calculated using 2.1 times the original number of digits. For arguments whose absolute value is 0.9 or less, "atanh(x) = 1/2 * log(1+x)/log(1-x)" is used, and outside that range it is calculated by Newton's method.However, when the number of digits is 10 or less, it is calculated using "the original number of digits plus 4" digits. This extra number of digits may be changed in the future, probably in the direction of higher precision.By using the symmetry of the function, negative arguments are calculated using the value for the corresponding positive argument.
In older versions, values could not be obtained for arguments less than or equal to -0.7, on the negative-infinity side. The calculation would not finish, or 0 would be returned. This problem was fixed in VCSSL 3.3.2.
function64.parameters: (varfloat type) arg : The value to be calculated.

function65.name: deg
function65.description: Converts an angle from radians to degrees.
function65.parameters: (float type) rad : The angle in radians.

function66.name: deg
function66.description:
Converts an angle from radians to degrees.
Internally, it is calculated using 1.34 times the original number of digits, by "radians = degrees * pi / 180".
function66.parameters: (varfloat type) rad : The angle in radians.
function66.example: digit(50); varfloat v = deg(pi()); print(v); // 179.999...9; print( round(v, 40v, HALF_UP) ); // 180.0

function67.name: rad
function67.description: Converts an angle from degrees to radians.
function67.parameters: (float type) deg : The angle in degrees.

function68.name: rad
function68.description:
Converts an angle from degrees to radians.
Internally, it is calculated using 1.34 times the original number of digits, by "degrees = radians * 180 / pi".
function68.parameters: (varfloat type) deg : The angle in degrees.
